Correct. Per Standard VI(A) Guidance on Disclosure of Conflicts to Employers: 'Members and candidates should take reasonable steps to avoid conflicts. However, if the conflicts are not resolved, they must report conflicts promptly.'

questions.stem: *Employer's restrictions to prevent appearance of conflicts* under VI(A) *most likely*:

options.A: many employers restrict PERSONAL TRADING, OUTSIDE BOARD MEMBERSHIP, and RELATED ACTIVITIES to prevent situations that might not normally be considered problematic from a conflict-of-interest point of view but that could give the appearance of a conflict of interest.
options.B: Employer restrictions on personal trading and outside board service exceed what the Code permits firms to impose.
options.C: Appearance-level conflicts need no management because the Code addresses only actual conflicts.

rationales.A:
Full statement: many employers restrict PERSONAL TRADING, OUTSIDE BOARD MEMBERSHIP, and RELATED ACTIVITIES to prevent situations that might not normally be considered problematic from a conflict-of-interest point of view but that could give the appearance of a conflict of interest; members and candidates MUST COMPLY with these employer restrictions.

Correct. Per Standard VI(A) Guidance: 'For example, many employers restrict personal trading, outside board membership, and related activities to prevent situations that might not normally be considered problematic from a conflict-of-interest point of view but that could give the appearance of a conflict of interest. Members and candidates must comply with these employer restrictions.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Firms legitimately restrict these activities, and members must comply.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Preventing even the APPEARANCE of conflict is the stated aim of such restrictions.

questions.stem: *Isolation of directors from investment decisions* under VI(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Director-members need no separation from investment decisions if they sign confidentiality undertakings.
options.B: when members and candidates providing investment services also serve as directors, they should be ISOLATED from those making investment decisions by FIREWALLS or similar restrictions.
options.C: Isolation of directors is required only during merger negotiations at the covered company.

rationales.A: Incorrect. FIREWALLS isolating the director role from investment decision-making are the recommended structure — undertakings alone do not stop information flow.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when members and candidates providing investment services also serve as directors, they should be ISOLATED from those making investment decisions by FIREWALLS or similar restrictions; this insulation manages the risk that MNPI acquired via board service could taint investment decisions.

Correct. Per Standard VI(A) Guidance on Conflicts as a Board Member or Director: 'When members and candidates providing investment services also serve as directors, they should be isolated from those making investment decisions by firewalls or similar restrictions.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The isolation is standing practice, not deal-contingent.

questions.stem: *Disclose to prospective clients too* under VI(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Conflict disclosure obligations run to current clients only; prospects can evaluate conflicts after signing.
options.B: Employers receive conflict disclosures; clients receive them only on request.
options.C: Standard VI(A) protects investors and employers by requiring members and candidates to avoid or fully disclose to CLIENTS, POTENTIAL CLIENTS, AND EMPLOYERS all actual and potential conflicts of interest.

rationales.A: Incorrect. POTENTIAL clients are express recipients — they need the information before engaging.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Clients, potential clients, AND employers all receive affirmative disclosure.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Standard VI(A) protects investors and employers by requiring members and candidates to avoid or fully disclose to CLIENTS, POTENTIAL CLIENTS, AND EMPLOYERS all actual and potential conflicts of interest; when a member or candidate has made full disclosure of an unavoidable conflict, the member's or candidate's employer, clients, and prospective clients will have the information needed to evaluate the objectivity of the investment advice or action taken on their behalf.

Correct. Per Standard VI(A) Guidance: 'Standard VI(A) protects investors and employers by requiring members and candidates to avoid or fully disclose to clients, potential clients, and employers all actual and potential conflicts of interest. When a member or candidate has made full disclosure of an unavoidable conflict, the member's or candidate's employer, clients, and prospective clients will have the information needed to evaluate the objectivity of the investment advice or action taken on their behalf.'

questions.stem: *Determination of disclosure frequency/manner* under VI(A) *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates are RESPONSIBLE FOR DETERMINING how often, in what manner, and in what particular circumstances the disclosure of conflicts must be made.
options.B: CFA Institute prescribes the exact frequency, format, and wording of conflict disclosures.
options.C: Disclosure frequency is set by each client in the advisory agreement.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates are RESPONSIBLE FOR DETERMINING how often, in what manner, and in what particular circumstances the disclosure of conflicts must be made; the standard does not prescribe a schedule; the member or candidate must exercise judgment consistent with the requirement that disclosures be prominent, in plain language, and effectively communicated.

Correct. Per Standard VI(A) Guidance: 'Members and candidates are responsible for determining how often, in what manner, and in what particular circumstances the disclosure of conflicts must be made.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The member is RESPONSIBLE FOR DETERMINING how often, in what manner, and in what circumstances to disclose.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Client agreements may inform practice, but the judgment and responsibility are the member's.

questions.stem: *Compliance recommendation — avoid altogether* under VI(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Best practice in Carter's situation was accepting the promoter's compensation with prompt disclosure.
options.B: in Example 6 (Carter), best practice was to AVOID the conflict altogether by DECLINING the offer of additional compensation from the promoter.
options.C: Carter's best course was donating the promoter's payments to charity while continuing the sales.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The illustrated best practice was AVOIDANCE — declining the additional compensation altogether.
rationales.B:
Full statement: in Example 6 (Carter), best practice was to AVOID the conflict altogether by DECLINING the offer of additional compensation from the promoter; the general recommendation is that when a conflict can be avoided rather than disclosed, avoidance is preferred.

Correct. Per Standard VI(A) Guidance: 'Best practice is to avoid actual conflicts of interest and the appearance of them.' Reinforced in Example 6 Comment: 'Best practice is for Carter to avoid this conflict of interest altogether by declining the offer of additional compensation from Badger Company.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Redirecting the money does not remove the incentive structure or the client's need to know; declining does.

questions.stem: *Discontinue coverage as compliance option* under VI(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Discontinuing coverage to escape a conflict is prohibited as a disservice to research clients.
options.B: The only remedy for an ownership conflict is selling the firm's stake.
options.C: per Example 2 (Hobbs/Dover & Roe), when a new ownership interest creates a conflict of interest, BEST PRACTICE would be to DISCONTINUE research coverage of the affected security.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Discontinuing coverage is the illustrated BEST-PRACTICE avoidance when new ownership creates the conflict.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Divestiture is one path, but coverage discontinuation (or prominent disclosure) is the practical remedy shown.
rationales.C:
Full statement: per Example 2 (Hobbs/Dover & Roe), when a new ownership interest creates a conflict of interest, BEST PRACTICE would be to DISCONTINUE research coverage of the affected security; if coverage continues, the ownership interest must be disclosed to all clients and required to be disclosed by portfolio managers when making specific investment recommendations or taking action on the security.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment (Hobbs): 'Best practice would be for Hobbs to discontinue research coverage of First of New York because the new ownership interest in this company creates a conflict of interest. At minimum, Hobbs must disclose the new relationship with First of New York to all Dover & Roe clients. Hobbs must also require the ownership interest to be disclosed to clients by the firm's portfolio managers when they make specific investment recommendations or take investment actions with respect to First of New York's securities.'

questions.stem: *Reassignment to another analyst* under VI(A) *most likely*:

options.A: per Example 3 (Fargmon), BEST PRACTICE is to AVOID THE CONFLICT by asking the employer to assign another analyst to draft the follow-up report.
options.B: Reassigning coverage to another analyst is inappropriate because it disrupts research continuity.
options.C: The conflicted analyst should retain the assignment and simply add a disclosure footnote.

rationales.A:
Full statement: per Example 3 (Fargmon), BEST PRACTICE is to AVOID THE CONFLICT by asking the employer to assign another analyst to draft the follow-up report; reassignment to a colleague not conflicted is a widely used avoidance mechanism when personal or family holdings would otherwise require ongoing disclosure and mitigation.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment (Fargmon): 'Best practice is to avoid the conflict by asking his employer to assign another analyst to draft the follow-up report.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Reassignment is the illustrated BEST PRACTICE — it avoids the conflict entirely.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Disclosure is the fallback; avoidance through reassignment is preferred when available.

questions.stem: *Firm personal-investing policy disclosure* under VI(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Firm personal-investing policies are internal documents that clients have no interest in seeing.
options.B: complying with an employer's policies and procedures that are designed to prevent potential conflicts of interest and even the appearance of a conflict of interest with respect to personal transactions is CRITICAL to establishing investor confidence in the investment industry.
options.C: Policy compliance substitutes for prominence — quiet adherence is all the standard asks.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members should encourage prominent disclosure of the firm's conflict-prevention policies to clients — they bear directly on client trust.
rationales.B:
Full statement: complying with an employer's policies and procedures that are designed to prevent potential conflicts of interest and even the appearance of a conflict of interest with respect to personal transactions is CRITICAL to establishing investor confidence in the investment industry; because investment firms vary greatly in AUM, types of clients, number of employees, and so on, members and candidates must carefully consider the policies regarding personal investing that are applicable to their situation; members and candidates should then PROMINENTLY DISCLOSE these policies to clients and prospective clients.

Correct. Per Standard VI(B) Compliance Practices: 'Complying with an employer's policies and procedures that are designed to prevent potential conflicts of interest and even the appearance of a conflict of interest with respect to personal transactions is critical to establishing investor confidence in the investment industry. Because investment firms vary greatly in assets under management, types of clients, number of employees, and so on, members and candidates must carefully consider the policies regarding personal investing that are applicable to their situation. Members and candidates should then prominently disclose these policies to clients and prospective clients.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Compliance with the policies AND appropriate disclosure both feature in the recommended practice.

questions.stem: *Limited participation in equity IPOs* under VI(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Investment personnel may participate in IPOs without restriction, as allocations are broker decisions.
options.B: IPO participation is problematic only when the offering is oversubscribed.
options.C: purchases of IPOs by investment personnel create conflicts of interest in TWO PRINCIPAL WAYS: FIRST, participation in an IPO may have the appearance of taking away an attractive investment opportunity from clients for personal gain — a clear breach of the duty of loyalty to clients.

rationales.A: Incorrect. IPO purchases by investment personnel raise appearance-of-favoritism and opportunity-appropriation conflicts; limits or preclearance are the practice.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The conflicts exist regardless of subscription levels.
rationales.C:
Full statement: purchases of IPOs by investment personnel create conflicts of interest in TWO PRINCIPAL WAYS: FIRST, participation in an IPO may have the appearance of taking away an attractive investment opportunity from clients for personal gain — a clear breach of the duty of loyalty to clients; SECOND, personal purchases in IPOs may have the appearance that the investment opportunity is being bestowed as an incentive to make future investment decisions for the benefit of the party providing the opportunity; members and candidates can avoid these conflicts or appearances of conflicts of interest by NOT PARTICIPATING in IPOs; when participation in IPOs is permitted by a firm's policies, members and candidates should PRECLEAR their participation in IPOs, EVEN IN SITUATIONS WITHOUT ANY CONFLICT OF INTEREST between a member's or candidate's participation in an IPO and the client's interests; members and candidates should NOT BENEFIT FROM THE POSITION THEIR CLIENTS OCCUPY in the marketplace — through preferred trading, the allocation of limited offerings, or oversubscription.

Correct. Per Standard VI(B) Compliance Practices: 'Limited participation in equity IPOs: Some eagerly awaited IPOs rise significantly in value shortly after the issue is brought to market... First, participation in an IPO may have the appearance of taking away an attractive investment opportunity from clients for personal gain — a clear breach of the duty of loyalty to clients. Second, personal purchases in IPOs may have the appearance that the investment opportunity is being bestowed as an incentive to make future investment decisions... Members and candidates can avoid these conflicts or appearances of conflicts of interest by not participating in IPOs. When participation in IPOs is permitted by a firm's policies, members and candidates should preclear their participation in IPOs, even in situations without any conflict of interest between a member's or candidate's participation in an IPO and the client's interests. Members and candidates should not benefit from the position that their clients occupy in the marketplace — through preferred trading, the allocation of limited offerings, or oversubscription.'

questions.stem: *Private placements* under VI(B) *most likely*:

options.A: employers may place STRICT LIMITS on investment personnel acquiring securities in private placements.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: employers may place STRICT LIMITS on investment personnel acquiring securities in private placements; participation in private placements raises conflict-of-interest issues that are SIMILAR to issues surrounding IPOs; investment personnel should NOT BE INVOLVED in transactions, including (but not limited to) private placements, that could be perceived as FAVORS OR GIFTS that seem designed to influence future judgment or to reward past business deals.

Correct. Per Standard VI(B) Compliance Practices: 'Restrictions on private placements: Employers may place strict limits on investment personnel acquiring securities in private placements. Participation in private placements raises conflict-of-interest issues that are similar to issues surrounding IPOs. Investment personnel should not be involved in transactions, including (but not limited to) private placements, that could be perceived as favors or gifts that seem designed to influence future judgment or to reward past business deals.'
rationales.B: Strict limits; not perceived as favors.
rationales.C: Private placement limits.

questions.stem: *Blackout/restricted periods* under VI(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Formal blackout periods are unnecessary where investment personnel individually certify in writing that they will not trade ahead of client transactions, since attestation achieves the same protection with less operational burden.
options.B: investment personnel involved in the investment decision-making process should comply with established BLACKOUT PERIODS so that managers cannot take advantage of their knowledge of client activity by 'FRONT-RUNNING' client trades (trading for one's personal account before trading for client accounts).
options.C: Blackout and restricted-period requirements apply only to the firm's most senior officers and portfolio managers, because junior personnel lack sufficient knowledge of pending transactions to front-run them.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Certifications do not substitute for structural blackout periods that prevent front-running opportunities.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Standard VI(B) Compliance Practices: 'Blackout/restricted periods: Investment personnel involved in the investment decision-making process should comply with established blackout periods so that managers cannot take advantage of their knowledge of client activity by "front-running" client trades (trading for one's personal account before trading for client accounts).'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Blackouts cover investment personnel involved in the DECISION-MAKING PROCESS, whatever their seniority.

questions.stem: *Reporting requirements — holdings disclosure* under VI(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Personal-holdings reporting is a voluntary courtesy members may decline on privacy grounds.
options.B: Reporting obligations cover trades only, never standing holdings or beneficial ownership.
options.C: members and candidates must comply with employer reporting policies, including DISCLOSURE OF PERSONAL HOLDINGS/BENEFICIAL OWNERSHIPS, confirmations of trades to the firm and the employee, and preclearance procedures.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members MUST comply with employer reporting policies, including holdings and beneficial-ownership disclosure.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Disclosure of HOLDINGS and beneficial ownership — at commencement and periodically — is part of the framework.
rationales.C:
Full statement: members and candidates must comply with employer reporting policies, including DISCLOSURE OF PERSONAL HOLDINGS/BENEFICIAL OWNERSHIPS, confirmations of trades to the firm and the employee, and preclearance procedures; disclosure of holdings in which the employee has a beneficial interest — firms may require disclosure upon COMMENCEMENT of the employment relationship and PERIODICALLY thereafter.

Correct. Per Standard VI(B) Compliance Practices: 'Disclosure of holdings in which the employee has a beneficial interest. Firms may require disclosure upon commencement of the employment relationship and periodically thereafter.'

questions.stem: *Duplicate confirmations of transactions* under VI(B) *most likely*:

options.A: firms may require employees to direct their brokers to supply to their employer DUPLICATE COPIES OR CONFIRMATIONS of all their personal securities transactions and copies of periodic statements for all securities accounts.
options.B: Duplicate confirmations are unnecessary because members self-report their trades.
options.C: Brokers are legally barred from sending trade confirmations to anyone but the account holder.

rationales.A:
Full statement: firms may require employees to direct their brokers to supply to their employer DUPLICATE COPIES OR CONFIRMATIONS of all their personal securities transactions and copies of periodic statements for all securities accounts; the duplicate confirmation requirement has TWO PURPOSES: (1) the requirement sends a message that there is INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION, which reduces the likelihood of unethical behavior, and (2) it enables VERIFICATION of the accounting of the flow of personal investments that cannot be determined from merely looking at holdings.

Correct. Per Standard VI(B) Compliance Practices: 'Providing duplicate confirmations of transactions. Firms may require employees to direct their brokers to supply to their employer duplicate copies or confirmations of all their personal securities transactions and copies of periodic statements for all securities accounts. The duplicate confirmation requirement has two purposes: (1) The requirement sends a message that there is independent verification, which reduces the likelihood of unethical behavior, and (2) it enables verification of the accounting of the flow of personal investments that cannot be determined from merely looking at holdings.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Independent broker-supplied duplicates verify self-reports and deter misconduct.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Employees can and do direct their brokers to supply duplicates to the employer; no bar exists.

questions.stem: *Preclearance procedures* under VI(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Trade review after execution provides the same protection as preclearance.
options.B: firms may require employees to disclose ALL PLANNED PERSONAL TRADES so that they may identify possible conflicts PRIOR TO THE EXECUTION of the trades.
options.C: Preclearance applies only to trades in securities on the firm's restricted list.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Preclearance identifies conflicts BEFORE execution — post-trade review cannot unwind a completed conflict.
rationales.B:
Full statement: firms may require employees to disclose ALL PLANNED PERSONAL TRADES so that they may identify possible conflicts PRIOR TO THE EXECUTION of the trades; preclearance procedures are designed to IDENTIFY POSSIBLE CONFLICTS BEFORE A PROBLEM ARISES.

Correct. Per Standard VI(B) Compliance Practices: 'Preclearance procedures. Firms may require employees to disclose all planned personal trades so that they may identify possible conflicts prior to the execution of the trades. Preclearance procedures are designed to identify possible conflicts before a problem arises.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The practice covers ALL planned personal trades, not just restricted names.

questions.stem: *Disclosure of policies vs boilerplate* under VI(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Boilerplate summaries of personal-investing policies satisfy investor requests.
options.B: Firms may decline investor requests about personal-investing policies as confidential.
options.C: upon request, members and candidates should FULLY DISCLOSE to investors their firm's policies regarding personal investing.

rationales.A: Incorrect. On request, the policies should be FULLY disclosed — generic boilerplate defeats the purpose.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Responsiveness to such requests is the recommended practice, not refusal.
rationales.C:
Full statement: upon request, members and candidates should FULLY DISCLOSE to investors their firm's policies regarding personal investing; the information about employees' personal investment activities and policies will foster an atmosphere of full and complete disclosure and address concerns about the conflicts of interest posed by personal trading; the disclosure must provide HELPFUL INFORMATION to investors; it should NOT be simply boilerplate language, such as 'investment personnel are subject to policies and procedures regarding their personal trading.'

Correct. Per Standard VI(B) Compliance Practices: 'Disclosure of policies: Upon request, members and candidates should fully disclose to investors their firm's policies regarding personal investing. The information about employees' personal investment activities and policies will foster an atmosphere of full and complete disclosure and address concerns about the conflicts of interest posed by personal trading. The disclosure must provide helpful information to investors; it should not be simply boilerplate language, such as "investment personnel are subject to policies and procedures regarding their personal trading."'

questions.stem: *Front-running definition* under VI(B) *most likely*:

options.A: FRONT-RUNNING client trades means trading for one's personal account BEFORE trading for client accounts.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: FRONT-RUNNING client trades means trading for one's personal account BEFORE trading for client accounts; blackout periods are designed to prevent managers from taking advantage of their knowledge of client activity by front-running client trades.

Correct. Per Standard VI(B) Compliance Practices: 'Blackout/restricted periods: Investment personnel involved in the investment decision-making process should comply with established blackout periods so that managers cannot take advantage of their knowledge of client activity by "front-running" client trades (trading for one's personal account before trading for client accounts).'
rationales.B: Personal ahead of clients.
rationales.C: Front-running defined.

questions.stem: *Sending message with independent verification* under VI(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Duplicate confirmations serve billing reconciliation and have no conduct dimension.
options.B: the duplicate confirmation requirement sends a MESSAGE that there is INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION, which REDUCES THE LIKELIHOOD of unethical behavior.
options.C: The verification message matters only for employees with prior violations.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The requirement signals INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION, which itself deters unethical behavior.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the duplicate confirmation requirement sends a MESSAGE that there is INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION, which REDUCES THE LIKELIHOOD of unethical behavior; verification serves both a deterrent function and a validation function for the flow of employee investments.

Correct. Per Standard VI(B) Compliance Practices: 'The duplicate confirmation requirement has two purposes: (1) The requirement sends a message that there is independent verification, which reduces the likelihood of unethical behavior, and (2) it enables verification of the accounting of the flow of personal investments that cannot be determined from merely looking at holdings.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The deterrent operates firm-wide, prospectively, on everyone covered.

questions.stem: *Family accounts — client treatment* under VI(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Family client accounts should receive systematically later allocations to preempt favoritism claims.
options.B: Family accounts may be favored modestly since relatives bear the member's reputational risk.
options.C: family accounts that are client accounts should be TREATED LIKE ANY OTHER FIRM ACCOUNT and should NEITHER be given special treatment NOR be disadvantaged because of the family relationship.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Disadvantaging family CLIENT accounts breaches the duty owed them — they are clients first.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Neither favoritism NOR disadvantage is permitted; family client accounts are treated like any other.
rationales.C:
Full statement: family accounts that are client accounts should be TREATED LIKE ANY OTHER FIRM ACCOUNT and should NEITHER be given special treatment NOR be disadvantaged because of the family relationship; if the member or candidate has beneficial ownership in a family account and the firm has preclearance and reporting requirements for personal transactions, the member or candidate may have to preclear the trades and report the transactions.

Correct. Per Standard VI(B) Guidance on Impact on Accounts with Beneficial Ownership: 'Family accounts that are client accounts should be treated like any other firm account and should neither be given special treatment nor be disadvantaged because of the family relationship.'

questions.stem: *Compliance with employer's personal-investing policies* under VI(B) *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates MUST COMPLY with their employer's personal investing policies, which could include: limited participation in equity IPOs.
options.B: Employer personal-investing policies are optional guidelines a member may weigh against convenience.
options.C: Only policies mirrored in securities regulation carry compliance force.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates MUST COMPLY with their employer's personal investing policies, which could include: limited participation in equity IPOs; restrictions on private placements; blackout/restricted periods; reporting requirements (holdings disclosure, duplicate confirmations, preclearance procedures); and disclosure of policies.

Correct. Per Standard VI(B) Compliance Practices: 'Members and candidates must comply with their employer's personal investing policies, which could include the following: Limited participation in equity IPOs... Restrictions on private placements... Blackout/restricted periods... Reporting requirements... Disclosure of policies...'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Compliance with the employer's established policies is MANDATORY under the standard.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Firm policies bind under IV(A) and VI(B) whether or not regulation duplicates them.

questions.stem: *Employer procedures for referral fees* under VI(C) *most likely*:

options.A: not recommended.
options.B: members and candidates should ENCOURAGE their employers to develop procedures related to referral fees.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Encourage procedures.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates should ENCOURAGE their employers to develop procedures related to referral fees; employers may COMPLETELY RESTRICT such fees or OUTLINE THE APPROPRIATE STEPS for requesting approval of a referral fee arrangement.

Correct. Per Standard VI(C) Compliance Practices: 'Members and candidates should encourage their employers to develop procedures related to referral fees. Employers may completely restrict such fees or outline the appropriate steps for requesting approval of a referral fee arrangement.'
rationales.C: Employer procedures.

questions.stem: *Regular updates on referral fees* under VI(C) *most likely*:

options.A: Referral-fee updates to the employer are due annually, aligned with compensation reviews.
options.B: Once approved, a referral program requires no further reporting to the employer.
options.C: members and candidates who participate in approved referral fee programs should provide to their employer REGULAR (AT LEAST QUARTERLY) UPDATES on the amount and nature of compensation received through referral fee arrangements.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Updates should be REGULAR — at least QUARTERLY.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Approval initiates, not ends, the reporting: quarterly updates on arrangements and compensation follow.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard VI(C) Compliance Practices: 'Members and candidates who participate in approved referral fee programs should provide to their employer regular (at least quarterly) updates on the amount and nature of compensation received through referral fee arrangements.'

questions.stem: *Consideration includes soft dollars* under VI(C) *most likely*:

options.A: consideration includes ALL FEES, whether paid in CASH, in SOFT DOLLARS, or IN KIND.
options.B: cash only.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: consideration includes ALL FEES, whether paid in CASH, in SOFT DOLLARS, or IN KIND; the disclosure must cover the full economic substance of the arrangement, not just monetary payments.

Correct. Per Standard VI(C) Guidance: 'Consideration includes all fees, whether paid in cash, in soft dollars, or in kind.'
rationales.B: Cash + soft dollars + in kind.
rationales.C: All consideration forms.

questions.stem: *Both inbound and outbound referral disclosure* under VI(C) *most likely*:

options.A: Only fees the member PAYS for referrals are disclosable; benefits received are private income.
options.B: members and candidates must inform their employer, clients, and prospective clients of any benefit RECEIVED for referrals of customers and clients.
options.C: Referral disclosure runs to the employer alone, never to clients.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Benefits RECEIVED for referrals must be disclosed to employer, clients, and prospects as appropriate.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates must inform their employer, clients, and prospective clients of any benefit RECEIVED for referrals of customers and clients; members and candidates must ALSO DISCLOSE when they PAY A FEE or provide compensation to OTHERS who have referred prospective clients to the member or candidate; both directions of referral compensation must be disclosed.

Correct. Per Standard VI(C) Guidance: 'Members and candidates must inform their employer, clients, and prospective clients of any benefit received for referrals of customers and clients... Members and candidates must also disclose when they pay a fee or provide compensation to others who have referred prospective clients to the member or candidate.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Clients and prospective clients are express recipients of the disclosure.

questions.stem: *Employer restrictions on referrals* under VI(C) *most likely*:

options.A: Employers may not restrict members' referral-fee arrangements, which are personal contracts.
options.B: Employer approval processes for referral fees violate members' freedom of association.
options.C: employers may COMPLETELY RESTRICT referral fees or outline the appropriate steps for requesting approval of a referral fee arrangement.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Employers may COMPLETELY RESTRICT referral fees or define the approval path.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Such processes are legitimate governance the member must follow.
rationales.C:
Full statement: employers may COMPLETELY RESTRICT referral fees or outline the appropriate steps for requesting approval of a referral fee arrangement; members and candidates must comply with such employer restrictions and pre-approval requirements.

Correct. Per Standard VI(C) Compliance Practices: 'Employers may completely restrict such fees or outline the appropriate steps for requesting approval of a referral fee arrangement.'

questions.stem: *Content of referral disclosure* under VI(C) *most likely*:

options.A: the member or candidate must disclose the NATURE of the consideration or benefit (flat fee or percentage basis, one-time or continuing benefit, benefit based on performance, benefit in the form of provision of research, or other noncash benefit) TOGETHER WITH the ESTIMATED DOLLAR VALUE.
options.B: The content of a referral disclosure is limited to identifying the referring party by name, since the commercial terms between the parties are not the referred client's concern.
options.C: Estimated dollar values should be omitted from referral disclosures when amounts are not yet fixed, because estimates are inherently speculative and risk misleading the client about the true cost.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the member or candidate must disclose the NATURE of the consideration or benefit (flat fee or percentage basis, one-time or continuing benefit, benefit based on performance, benefit in the form of provision of research, or other noncash benefit) TOGETHER WITH the ESTIMATED DOLLAR VALUE; consideration includes all fees, whether paid in cash, in soft dollars, or in kind.

Correct. Per Standard VI(C) Guidance: 'The member or candidate must disclose the nature of the consideration or benefit — for example, flat fee or percentage basis, one-time or continuing benefit, benefit based on performance, benefit in the form of provision of research, or other noncash benefit — together with the estimated dollar value. Consideration includes all fees, whether paid in cash, in soft dollars, or in kind.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The NATURE of the consideration — structure, duration, benefit type — must be conveyed.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Where the amount is not fixed, an ESTIMATED value should be disclosed rather than nothing.

questions.stem: *Interdepartmental referrals — same rule* under VI(C) *most likely*:

options.A: Payments between departments of the same firm are internal transfers outside VI(C).
options.B: Standard VI(C) does NOT distinguish between referral payments paid by a third party for referring clients to the third party and INTERNAL PAYMENTS paid within a firm to attract new business to a subsidiary.
options.C: Interdepartmental referral credit is disclosable only if it exceeds external referral rates.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The standard does NOT distinguish third-party payments from INTERNAL payments between departments.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard VI(C) does NOT distinguish between referral payments paid by a third party for referring clients to the third party and INTERNAL PAYMENTS paid within a firm to attract new business to a subsidiary; members and candidates must disclose ALL such referral fees; the disclosure must include the nature and the value of the benefit and must occur AT THE TIME OF THE REFERRAL.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment (Handley): 'Standard VI(C) does not distinguish between referral payments paid by a third party for referring clients to the third party and internal payments paid within a firm to attract new business to a subsidiary. Members and candidates must disclose all such referral fees. Therefore, Handley is required to disclose, at the time of referral, any referral fee agreement in place among Central Trust Bank's departments. The disclosure must include the nature and the value of the benefit.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. No rate threshold exists; internal referral benefits are disclosable on the same basis as external ones.

questions.stem: *Purpose of referral fee disclosure — two reasons* under VI(C) *most likely*:

options.A: The single purpose of referral disclosure is deterring referral arrangements altogether.
options.B: Referral disclosures exist to standardize fee levels across the industry.
options.C: such disclosures allow clients and employers to evaluate (1) any PARTIALITY shown in any recommendation of products or services and (2) the FULL COST of the services.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Referrals are legitimate; the two purposes are evaluating PARTIALITY and FULL COST.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Price standardization is not a purpose; informed client evaluation is.
rationales.C:
Full statement: such disclosures allow clients and employers to evaluate (1) any PARTIALITY shown in any recommendation of products or services and (2) the FULL COST of the services; these two purposes are the primary rationale for VI(C) disclosure.

Correct. Per Standard VI(C) Guidance: 'Such disclosures allow clients and employers to evaluate (1) any partiality shown in any recommendation of products or services and (2) the full cost of the services.'
